summary: A 68-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 32%), NYHA class II symptoms, no diabetes, on optimal medical therapy (sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone) asks his nurse practitioner about adding dapagliflozin even though he is not diabetic. The held-out answer must reflect the AHA/ACC/HFSA 2022 heart-failure guideline recommendation of an SGLT2 inhibitor as a fourth pillar of GDMT regardless of diabetes status, the DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) trial findings, the genitourinary side-effect profile, the rare euglycemic DKA caveat (more relevant in DM2), and the volume-status / dose-adjustment considerations.

messages.content: I have heart failure — my ejection fraction is 32%. I'm on Entresto, carvedilol, and spironolactone. My NP wants to add dapagliflozin. But I'm not diabetic. Why am I getting a diabetes drug?

do_NOT: describe SGLT2 inhibitors as DM-only drugs
skip the heart-failure mortality / hospitalization benefit
ignore the rare euglycemic DKA in non-diabetic HF context (low but reportable)
describe SGLT2 as a substitute for the existing GDMT pillars

expected_sources_referenced: AHA/ACC/HFSA 2022 Heart Failure Guideline — Heidenreich et al., J Am Coll Cardiol 2022
